choice_d: Because default routes are only for hosts

source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why is a host route useful when an administrator wants to affect only one destination?
choice_a: Because it can target one exact host without changing the whole subnet route
choice_b: Because it disables the default route
choice_c: Because it automatically summarizes networks
choice_d: Because it forces BGP to advertise
explanation: A host route changes forwarding for one destination without changing the larger subnet's path.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why is using a next-hop IP often clearer than naming only an exit interface on a multi-access network?
choice_a: Because it identifies the intended neighboring router more explicitly
choice_b: Because interface-based routes cannot forward packets
choice_c: Because exit interfaces remove administrative distance
choice_d: Because it changes the frame check sequence
explanation: A next-hop IP makes the intended path clearer when multiple devices share a segment.

question: Why is a connected route treated as highly trustworthy?
choice_a: Because the network is directly attached to the router
choice_b: Because it has the largest subnet mask
choice_c: Because it is learned from the internet
choice_d: Because it has the highest possible metric
explanation: Connected networks are directly reachable through the router's own interface.

question: Why can ARP not by itself determine the path to a remote network?
choice_a: Because ARP resolves only local MAC addressing and not Layer 3 path choice
choice_b: Because ARP only works on routers
choice_c: Because ARP replaces the routing table
choice_d: Because ARP carries subnet masks only
explanation: ARP solves local delivery, but routing decides how to reach remote networks.

question: Why can a routing table not by itself deliver a frame on Ethernet?
choice_a: Because the device still needs the correct local MAC address
choice_b: Because the routing table contains no IP networks
choice_c: Because Layer 3 devices do not use frames
choice_d: Because switches do not allow route lookups
explanation: A routing decision still requires local Layer 2 delivery information such as a destination MAC.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why is the default gateway MAC used for remote traffic rather than the final remote host MAC?
choice_a: Because Layer 2 delivery only reaches the next local hop
choice_b: Because the remote host does not have an IP address
choice_c: Because the gateway rewrites DNS records
choice_d: Because ARP can cross every router
explanation: A host can only send a frame across its local link, so the frame must target the next hop.

question: Why does a router remove the old Layer 2 header when forwarding a packet?
choice_a: Because the old Layer 2 header was valid only for the previous hop
choice_b: Because the IP header can never be forwarded
choice_c: Because ARP entries expire immediately
choice_d: Because switches require empty frames
explanation: The incoming Layer 2 header served only the prior local link and must be replaced for the next link.

question: Why can the MAC addresses change at each routed hop while the IP addresses stay focused on the endpoints?
choice_a: Because Layer 2 is hop-local while Layer 3 is end-to-end
choice_b: Because routers rewrite IP addresses every time
choice_c: Because MAC addresses are larger than IP addresses
choice_d: Because ARP changes subnet masks
explanation: Layer 2 addresses are local to each link, but Layer 3 addresses identify the endpoints across the path.

question: Why does a packet usually pause before transmission if the needed MAC address is unknown?
choice_a: Because ARP resolution must complete before the frame can be built
choice_b: Because the router is recomputing subnet masks
choice_c: Because DHCP must assign a new address first
choice_d: Because the switch disables the port briefly

source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why are Layer 2 checks done before a router makes its Layer 3 forwarding decision?
choice_a: Because the router must first accept the incoming frame as valid on that interface
choice_b: Because Layer 3 always happens on switches first
choice_c: Because routing tables contain MAC addresses only
choice_d: Because ARP cannot run until TTL is checked
explanation: The router first confirms the frame is valid and intended for that interface before processing the IP packet inside it.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why does successful routed communication depend on both Layer 2 and Layer 3 processes?
choice_a: Because Layer 3 chooses the path and Layer 2 handles each local hop
choice_b: Because Layer 2 alone can route between subnets
choice_c: Because Layer 3 alone builds Ethernet frames
choice_d: Because only Layer 1 matters after the first hop
explanation: End-to-end delivery requires routing decisions plus local frame delivery at every hop.

question: Why are IGPs described as chatty compared with EGPs?
choice_a: Because they exchange information more aggressively to react quickly to internal failures
choice_b: Because they send only one update per day
choice_c: Because they do not use routing tables
choice_d: Because they operate at Layer 2
explanation: IGPs trade extra internal communication for faster responsiveness.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why is BGP usually not the first choice for fast internal failure response?
choice_a: Because BGP emphasizes stability and policy more than rapid internal convergence
choice_b: Because BGP cannot carry routes
choice_c: Because BGP does not use IP networks
choice_d: Because BGP is only for switches
explanation: BGP is designed for controlled inter-domain behavior rather than quick internal convergence.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/rocket-questions-html/notes-content/MASTER LIST/Weekly AI Master Lists/Week 11 - AI Master List.md

question: Why might the wider internet be intentionally shielded from small internal failures inside one organization?
choice_a: Because propagating every small event everywhere would create unnecessary instability
choice_b: Because the internet does not use IP routing
choice_c: Because autonomous systems cannot fail internally
choice_d: Because default routes prevent all changes
explanation: EGPs such as BGP limit how much internal instability is exposed to the rest of the internet.

question: Why is policy control especially important between autonomous systems?
choice_a: Because organizations may need to control what routes they accept and advertise
choice_b: Because policy replaces routing tables
choice_c: Because autonomous systems share one MAC table
choice_d: Because only BGP uses subnet masks
explanation: Between organizations, routing decisions involve trust, filtering, and business policy.

question: Why can BGP help prevent an organization from advertising routes it should not claim?
choice_a: Because neighboring networks can filter advertisements based on expected ownership and policy
choice_b: Because BGP ignores all route announcements
choice_c: Because BGP removes next-hop information
choice_d: Because ARP validates every external prefix
explanation: BGP supports route filtering and policy checks between autonomous systems.

question: Why can an organization use both an IGP and BGP at the same time?
choice_a: Because one can handle internal routing while the other handles external route exchange
choice_b: Because BGP replaces local forwarding
choice_c: Because IGPs cannot run on routers
choice_d: Because only one protocol can carry default routes
explanation: Many networks use an IGP internally and BGP at the edge toward other autonomous systems.

question: Why does the concept of an autonomous system matter when choosing routing protocols?
choice_a: Because protocol choice depends in part on whether routing is staying inside one administrative domain or crossing into another
choice_b: Because every subnet is its own autonomous system
choice_c: Because autonomous systems eliminate next hops
choice_d: Because ARP creates autonomous systems automatically

question: Why are route filtering and preference usually discussed more with BGP than with basic IGP behavior?
choice_a: Because BGP is built for policy-rich decisions between organizations
choice_b: Because IGPs cannot carry more than one route
choice_c: Because BGP ignores administrative distance
choice_d: Because IGPs never use metrics
explanation: BGP is heavily policy-driven compared with the simpler internal goals of many IGPs.

question: Why does the internet rely on BGP between providers?
choice_a: Because providers need a way to exchange routes between many separate autonomous systems with control and policy
choice_b: Because BGP is a Layer 2 loop-prevention protocol
choice_c: Because providers cannot use IP addresses
choice_d: Because BGP automatically replaces all static routes inside every LAN
explanation: The internet is formed by many connected autonomous systems that exchange routes primarily with BGP.

question: Why do link-state protocols usually need more RAM and CPU than distance-vector protocols?
choice_a: Because they maintain more complete topology information
choice_b: Because they disable route aggregation
choice_c: Because they have no routing tables
choice_d: Because they rewrite every IP header

question: Why do link-state protocols often converge faster after a failure?
choice_a: Because they may already know an alternate path through the topology
choice_b: Because they never need next hops
choice_c: Because they use no CPU at all
choice_d: Because they always use static routes
explanation: Broader topology knowledge lets link-state protocols react more quickly to path changes.

question: Why can distance-vector protocols converge more slowly after a failure?

choice_c: Because they have no route source trust
choice_d: Because they cannot identify next hops

question: Why can a distance-vector router temporarily lose all known paths to a destination after a failure?

choice_b: Because MAC addresses are no longer valid globally
choice_c: Because the default gateway disappears from every subnet
choice_d: Because IP addressing stops working

question: Why is full-topology knowledge valuable in redundant networks?

choice_c: Because it disables administrative distance
choice_d: Because it prevents all route changes

question: Why might an older low-resource router favor a simpler protocol category?

choice_c: Because simple protocols do not need routing tables
choice_d: Because older routers cannot have next hops

question: Why is the RAM and CPU penalty of link-state protocols described as less important on modern routers?
choice_a: Because newer routers usually have far more resources than older hardware

choice_c: Because modern routers do not use metrics
choice_d: Because distance-vector protocols were removed from certification

question: Why is EIGRP sometimes described as hybrid or advanced distance-vector?

choice_d: Because it has no convergence process

question: Why is it inaccurate to say one routing category is always better than the other?
choice_a: Because design choices depend on topology, resources, and operational priorities

explanation: Protocol choice depends on tradeoffs such as convergence speed, control, scale, and resource use.

question: Why do routers need both a destination network and a next-hop decision for remote traffic?

question: Why is static routing considered controlled but not adaptive?
choice_a: Because it follows administrator intent exactly but does not learn changes automatically

choice_c: Because it depends on switch floods
choice_d: Because it removes the routing table

question: Why can a default route be especially useful toward an upstream network?
choice_a: Because many unknown destinations can be sent to a single upstream exit

choice_c: Because it works only on hosts

question: Why does an autonomous system represent an administrative boundary rather than just a physical one?

question: Why are static routing and dynamic routing often treated as complementary rather than mutually exclusive?

question: Why is a host's default gateway often described as the exit point from the local subnet?

question: Why can return traffic follow a different path than outbound traffic in routed networks?
choice_a: Because routing decisions are made independently in each direction

choice_c: Because switches require mirrored forwarding

question: Why does manual backup design matter in static routing?

question: Why is it useful to study static routing before moving into dynamic routing?

question: When multiple routes match a destination, which decision is generally made before administrative distance is compared?

question: When two routes have the same destination prefix length but come from different sources, what commonly determines which one is preferred?

question: What concept describes a router having to resolve the next hop of a route by using another route?

question: What is a common purpose of a static null route?

choice_c: To speed up ARP replies
choice_d: To elect a root bridge
explanation: A static null route is often used to discard certain traffic intentionally.

question: What does equal-cost multipath refer to?

choice_c: Sending every frame twice by design

question: What risk can come from overly broad route summarization?

question: What is route poisoning in distance-vector routing?
choice_a: Advertising a failed route as unreachable

choice_c: Removing administrative distance from a route

question: What problem is split horizon designed to reduce in distance-vector routing?

question: What is classless routing?

explanation: Classless routing uses prefixes and longest-prefix match instead of relying on historical address classes.

question: What routing failure can occur if a router's default route points to a next-hop device that cannot actually reach the destination network?

choice_c: The router becomes a switch

question: What is the purpose of injecting a default route into a dynamic routing protocol?

question: Why can a static route disappear from the routing table if its next hop is not resolvable?

question: What is one purpose of tracking tied to a floating static route?

question: What does load balancing mean in a routing context?

choice_c: Assigning one VLAN to every route
choice_d: Changing source IPs on every hop
explanation: Load balancing uses more than one path when the routing logic allows it.

question: Why can summarization reduce the visibility of individual route failures outside the summarizing router?

choice_b: Because ARP suppresses all route changes
choice_c: Because summaries disable convergence
choice_d: Because a summary removes all next hops

question: Why is it valuable to understand both in-scope routing basics and adjacent certification-level routing behavior?
choice_a: Because foundational routing decisions connect directly to more advanced path control, scale, and troubleshooting topics

question: Why is the default gateway often the most important route-related setting on a basic host?
choice_a: Because it gives the host a path to destinations outside the local subnet
choice_b: Because it replaces the host IP address
choice_c: Because it creates connected routes on routers
choice_d: Because it disables ARP broadcasts
explanation: Without a default gateway, a basic host normally cannot send traffic beyond its own local network.

question: Why does automatic route sharing reduce administrative workload in larger networks?
choice_a: Because routers can learn many remote paths without each one being typed manually
choice_b: Because it removes the need for subnet masks
choice_c: Because it turns every route into a default route
choice_d: Because it prevents all route changes
explanation: Dynamic routing reduces the amount of manual per-route configuration required as scale increases.

question: Why is protocol selection always tied to network goals rather than only to protocol popularity?
choice_a: Because the best choice depends on scale, control needs, convergence priorities, and hardware context
choice_b: Because popular protocols never change
choice_c: Because every protocol produces identical behavior
choice_d: Because routing works the same in every topology

question: What is the key difference between administrative distance and a routing metric?
choice_a: Administrative distance compares route sources, while a metric compares paths within a route source
choice_b: Administrative distance measures ARP speed, while a metric measures MTU
choice_c: Administrative distance is Layer 2, while a metric is Layer 1
choice_d: Administrative distance applies only to switches, while a metric applies only to routers
explanation: Administrative distance ranks where a route came from, while metrics are used within a routing source to compare path quality.
